g.text: Formal Language Hierarchy: Where the Lexer Ends and the Parser Begins
Concrete Examples: Same Token, Different Territory
What the Lexer CANNOT Do (by mathematical necessity)
((( — lexer emits 3 LPAREN tokens, no error
Unbounded counter = not a DFA = not regular
Fix: parser checks balance using call stack
Cannot: Detect keyword used as variable
if = 42; — lexer emits KEYWORD ASSIGN NUMBER
Legality of assignment to keyword is semantic
Fix: semantic analysis checks symbol table
/* a /* b */ — lexer closes at FIRST */
Nesting would need depth counter (not regular)
Design choice: non-nesting keeps comments regular
Why the Lexer Stops at Regular Languages
DFA: finite states, no stack, no backtracking
Each char visited EXACTLY ONCE by advance()
This is NOT a limitation — it is a guarantee
Lexer handles: flat patterns, no counting, no nesting
Signal: if you need a counter -> parser territory
Signal: if you need a stack -> parser territory
Signal: if a loop suffices -> lexer territory
Balanced parens NOT regular — proved by Pumping Lemma
Assume DFA with k states recognizes balanced parens
Feed (^(k+1) )^(k+1) — by pigeonhole, a state repeats
Pump the repeated segment -> unbalanced string accepted
Contradiction. Balanced parens is context-free, not regular
Each token: one forward pass, no backtracking
Parser receives token stream, builds AST
IfStmt → KEYWORD('if') LPAREN Expr RPAREN Block
Block → LBRACE Stmt* RBRACE ← recursive rule
Parser CHECKS: does LPAREN match RPAREN?
Parser CHECKS: does LBRACE match RBRACE?
Parser uses call STACK for recursive rules
Unbalanced { } → parser error, NOT lexer error
Lexer emits all tokens even if unbalanced
Type checking: variable declared before use
Overload resolution: which f() to call
{ stmt* } ← brace blocks — arbitrary nesting depth
if cond { A } else { B } ← nested if/else matching
expr := term ((+ | -) term)* ← recursive grammar rules
Why CF? Matching brackets needs unbounded stack memory
Regular Constructs (DFA / finite state — no stack needed)
KEYWORD fixed set lookup in dict e.g. if, while, return
Why Regular? DFA has FINITE states — no counter, no stack
Complexity: O(n) time, O(1) extra memory — linear scan
